title: checkpointing a container requires root
body:
Although https://podman.io/blogs/2018/10/10/checkpoint-restore.html has examples of checkpoint run as user, on podman-0.12.1.2-1.git9551f6b.fc29.x86_64 it does not work:
$ podman container checkpoint teamviewer10
checkpointing a container requires root

comments.body:
I am not sure if we have rootless support for check pointing.

Could you try this with running podman as root.
I'm pretty sure we should just change the examples here - @adrianreber can confirm, but I think we have a requirement for root with checkpoint/restore

checkpoint works as root, restore no:
```
# podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                COMMAND  CREATED         STATUS             PORTS  NAMES
8e802732b330  quay.io/nerijus/teamviewer10:latest  bash     12 seconds ago  Up 12 seconds ago         amazing_yalow
# podman container checkpoint 8e802732b330
8e802732b3309582e8d36af03f639decfb88a1ffcce2b6b8ee9a09e4ee041880
# podman container restore 8e802732b330
unable to find container 8e802732b330: no container with name or ID 8e802732b330 found: no such container
```

Checkpointing can only work as root as CRIU has no support for non-root checkpointing. Some years ago there was a push to get it working as non root, but right now development is stuck upstream.

There are a few kernel interfaces missing to get it working as non-root. It is doable, but right now I am not aware of anybody in the CRIU community actively working on it.

Google is using CRIU for container live migration internally as non-root: https://linuxplumbersconf.org/event/2/contributions/209/attachments/27/24/Task_Migration_at_Google_Using_CRIU.pdf

One of the main problems is that restoring a process with the same PID requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN.
@nerijus Where do you see that it works as non-root?

https://podman.io/blogs/2018/10/10/checkpoint-restore.html :

"I can now checkpoint the container:
$ podman container checkpoint podman-criu-test"
@nerijus hm, okay, sorry if it confusing, but this should not mean as non-root. Why does this mean as non-root? What would be better to make it clear it works only as root?
Because of prompt - $. It ususally means user, # means root.
Ah, right. Did not think about that. Good point for the future. Thanks.

> checkpoint works as root, restore no:
> 
> ```
> # podman ps
> CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                COMMAND  CREATED         STATUS             PORTS  NAMES
> 8e802732b330  quay.io/nerijus/teamviewer10:latest  bash     12 seconds ago  Up 12 seconds ago         amazing_yalow
> # podman container checkpoint 8e802732b330
> 8e802732b3309582e8d36af03f639decfb88a1ffcce2b6b8ee9a09e4ee041880
> # podman container restore 8e802732b330
> unable to find container 8e802732b330: no container with name or ID 8e802732b330 found: no such container
> ```

Can you run ` podman ps -a` to share the state of the checkpointed container.

Can you run `podman container checkpoint -k 8e802732b330`. With `-k` there should be a file `dump.log` and a `checkpoint` directory. Can you share the log file? If you run podman with `--log-level debug` there should be a line where `dump.log` is mentioned. Something like:
```
Writing checkpoint logs to /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/e6463dc939fdcdf895fc549f907d4f825c9a3d1e9197240844b892a940a0361f/userdata 
```

Maybe also a listing of the content of the `checkpoint` directory.

```
# podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                COMMAND  CREATED        STATUS            PORTS  NAMES
6767c3669a50  quay.io/nerijus/teamviewer10:latest  bash     2 seconds ago  Up 2 seconds ago         tv10
# podman container checkpoint -k tv10
6767c3669a50630dc3cc8df95acb10a7db8ae41214b3ae655c30d18c7a4d163e
# ls -l dump.log  checkpoint
ls: cannot access 'dump.log': No such file or directory
ls: cannot access 'checkpoint': No such file or directory
# podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE  COMMAND  CREATED  STATUS  PORTS  NAMES
```

There is no "Writing checkpoint logs" with --log-level debug. After checkpoint I see this:
```
DEBU[0033] Checking container d379c38c493ed7ec681cba659baa26f9be9fea9ab52e7999159d14633cba0320 status... 
DEBU[0033] Cleaning up container d379c38c493ed7ec681cba659baa26f9be9fea9ab52e7999159d14633cba0320 
DEBU[0033] Network is already cleaned up, skipping...   
DEBU[0033] Storage is already unmounted, skipping...    
DEBU[0033] Storage is already unmounted, skipping...    
DEBU[0034] [graphdriver] trying provided driver "overlay" 
DEBU[0034] overlay test mount with multiple lowers succeeded 
DEBU[0034] backingFs=extfs, projectQuotaSupported=false, useNativeDiff=true 
```

Interesting. Not sure what is happening, yet.

Which distribution do you have, which version of Podman and which version of CRIU?

On F29 I see the following:
```

6f724578a797  docker.io/library/alpine:latest  top      31 seconds ago  Up 31 seconds ago         focused_carson
[root@fedora29 ~]#

If you try those commands, does that work? In this case the log file would be at: `/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/6f724578a7972c75f5cfe848ec0ef6c71cec13e4b6190f5d7d63565bcf318e30/userdata/dump.log`

It works, I get
```
DEBU[0000] Writing checkpoint to /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/9657e8d4443b9fa534591d5b38404b6e6c3325854dc96f18f49b25ca3c9c602e/userdata/checkpoint 
DEBU[0000] Writing checkpoint logs to /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/9657e8d4443b9fa534591d5b38404b6e6c3325854dc96f18f49b25ca3c9c602e/userdata 
```
but there is no dump.log:
```
# ls -l /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/9657e8d4443b9fa534591d5b38404b6e6c3325854dc96f18f49b25ca3c9c602e/userdata/dump*
ls: cannot access '/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/9657e8d4443b9fa534591d5b38404b6e6c3325854dc96f18f49b25ca3c9c602e/userdata/dump*': No such file or directory
```

`dump.log` is only kept if you are running the `checkpoint` command with `-k` (or `--keep`).

But now it at least sounds like basic checkpoint/restore works on your system. Good. Can you now try the same commands with your container to see if that also works. This time please use `-k`, so that I can see the `dump.log` in case it fails. Please always include all the output from `podman  --log-level debug` for the checkpoint and restore command.

```
# podman run -ti -d --tmpfs /run --tmpfs /tmp --name tv10 \
       -e DISPLAY=$DISPLAY \
       -v /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix \
       -v /home/nerijus/.Xauthority:/home/tv/.Xauthority:ro \
       -v /etc/machine-id:/etc/machine-id:ro \
       -v /home/nerijus/.config/teamviewer10:/home/tv/.config/teamviewer10 \
       -v /opt/teamviewer10:/opt/teamviewer \
       teamviewer10 bash
ae0f769a71bb5660af66c017fcf89814b7c7131d9322f83e2009ca81ea879e35
```
```
# podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                COMMAND  CREATED        STATUS            PORTS  NAMES

Cool, so checkpointing works. It now fails in restore. Unfortunately the restore creates another log file. If you could also share `restore.log` which should be available right next to `dump.log` after the restore command has failed.
https://sat.lt/restore.lo
@avagin do you have an idea why the restore does not work? Have you seen errors like this before?
I think there was a recent runc bug fix. Which version of runc are you using? This could be https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/pull/1944

> @adrianreber isn't `criu` not supposed to dump/restore the network namespace?

Too many negations in one sentence :wink: ; not sure I understand your question, but I think the runc version is new enough to contain the correct network namespace handling in combination with CRIU.

I tried to build an updated version of runc with the missing patch from https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/pull/1944 included:

https://koji.fedoraproject.org/koji/taskinfo?taskID=31556170

@nerijus can you rerun the restore with the updated runc package from the link above.
With updated runc restore worked successfully.

Perfect! Thanks @nerijus for testing, so can this be closed? Now that it works?
